en.extract:
The black-banded crake (Rufirallus fasciatus) is a species of bird in subfamily Rallinae of family Rallidae, the rails, gallinules, and coots. It is found in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. The species was formerly placed in the genus Laterallus.


Taxonomy
The black-banded crake was formally described in 1868 by the English ornithologists Philip Sclater and Osbert Salvin based a specimen collected in eastern Peru. They placed the species in the genus Porzana and coined the binomial name Porzana fasciata. The closest relatives of the black-banded crake were uncertain and the species was variously placed in the genera Porzana, Laterallus and Anurolimnas. Based on a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2023 the black-banded crake is now placed with four other crakes in the genus Rufirallus that had been introduced in 1856 by the French naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte. The genus name combines the Latin rufus meaning "rufous" with the genus Rallus that had been introduced by Carl Linnaeus in 1758 for the rails. The specific epithet fasciatus is Late Latin meaning "banded", from Latin fascia meaning "band" or "stripe".. The species is considered to be monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The black-banded crake is 17 to 20 cm (6.7 to 7.9 in) long. The sexes are alike. Adults have a dark horn or blackish bill. Their head, throat, and breast are rufous. Their back, rump, and wings are deep olive-brown, with a light reddish tinge to the inner flight feathers. Their belly, vent, and undertail coverts are cinnamon-rufous with heavy black bars. Their legs and feet are bright coral red. Immatures have a paler head and breast than adults. Their upperparts' olive-brown has a chestnut wash and the barring on their underparts is olive-brown. One author described the black-banded crake's song as "a rubbery, musical trill, deeper and more bubbly than that of [other] Laterallus crakes." The female sings a shorter version of the song than the male.


Distribution and habitat
The black-banded crake is found in the western Amazon Basin, from southeastern Colombia south through eastern Ecuador to south-central Peru and east into western Brazil. It inhabits humid landscapes with a dense understory such as secondary forest, where it especially favors overgrown agricultural plots in tropical evergreen forest. It also favors thickets of Heliconia and, on river islands, stands of Cecropia. In elevation it is mostly found below 600 m (2,000 ft) but reaches as high as 1,100 m (3,600 ft) locally in Ecuador. The black-banded crake is a year-round resident throughout its range.


Behavior


Feeding
The black-banded crake forages on damp ground in dense cover, where it searches for food in leaf litter. Its diet has not been documented but is assumed to be invertebrates and seeds.


Breeding
Almost nothing is known about the black-banded crake's breeding biology. It vigorously defends territories of up to about 2 ha (5 acres). It makes a dome nest of grass with a side entrance. One was sited on a fallen limb about 2 m (7 ft) above the ground.


Conservation status
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed the black-banded crake as being of Least Concern. It has a large range but its population size and trend are not known. No immediate threats have been identified. Authors describe it as "rare to locally fairly common" in Ecuador and "rare but widespread" in Peru. The species "may even benefit from low levels of human disturbance, taking advantage of edge habitats around small garden plots."


Notes


References

The '''black-banded crake''' ('''''Rufirallus fasciatus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in subfamily Rallinae of family [[Rallidae]], the rails, gallinules, and coots. It is found in [[Brazil]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], and [[Peru]]. The species was formerly placed in the genus ''[[Laterallus]]''.

==Taxonomy==
The black-banded crake was [[species description|formally described]] in 1868 by the English ornithologists [[Philip Sclater]] and [[Osbert Salvin]] based a specimen collected in eastern Peru. They placed the species in the genus ''[[Porzana]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Porzana fasciata''.<ref>{{ cite journal |last1=Sclater | first1=P.L. | author1-link=Philip Sclater | last2=Salvin | first2=O. | author2-link=Osbert Salvin | date=1867 | publication-date=1868 | title=List of birds collected at Pebas, Upper Amazons, by Mr. John Hauxwell, with notes and descriptions of new species | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | pages=977-981 [981] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/29534423 }}</ref>{{efn|Sclater and Salvin described the black-banded crake in a later volume of the journal under the binomial name ''Porzana hauxwelli''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Sclater | first1=P.L. | author1-link=Philip Sclater | last2=Salvin | first2=O. | author2-link=Osbert Salvin | year=1868 | publication-date=1869 | title=Synopsis of the American rails (Rallidae) | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | pages=442–470 [453] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28665004 }}</ref> The American ornithologist [[James L. Peters]] in the second volume of his ''Check-List of Birds of the World'' used this name rather than the earlier ''Porzana fasciatus''.<ref name=H&M4/><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1934 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=2 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=192 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483005 }}</ref>}} The closest relatives of the black-banded crake were uncertain and the species was variously placed in the genera ''[[Porzana]]'', ''[[Laterallus]]'' and ''[[Anurolimnas]]''.<ref name=H&M4>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Dickinson | editor1-first=E.C. | editor1-link=Edward C. Dickinson | editor2-last=Remsen | editor2-first=J.V. Jr. | editor2-link=James Van Remsen Jr. | year=2013 | title=The Howard & Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World | volume=1: Non-passerines | edition=4th | location=Eastbourne, UK | publisher=Aves Press | isbn=978-0-9568611-0-8 | page=157 | url=https://archive.org/details/howardmoorecompl0001howa/page/157/mode/1up | url-access=registration }}</ref><ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Taylor | first=P.B. | year=1996 | chapter=Family Rallidae (Rails, Gallinules and coots) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=3: Hoatzin to Auks | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-20-7 | pages=108-209 [156-157] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0003unse/page/156/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Flufftails, finfoots, rails, trumpeters, cranes, limpkin | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flufftails/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=2 March 2025}}</ref> Based on a [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2023<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Depino | first1=E.A. | last2=Pérez-Emán | first2=J.L. | last3=Bonaccorso | first3=E. | last4=Areta | first4=J.I. | date=2023 | title=Evolutionary history of New World crakes (Aves: Rallidae) with emphasis on the tribe Laterallini | journal=Zoologica Scripta | volume=52 | issue=4 | doi=10.1111/zsc.12595}}</ref> the black-banded crake is now placed with four other crakes in the genus ''[[Rufirallus]]'' that had been introduced in 1856 by the French naturalist [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte]].<ref name=ioc/> The genus name combines the [[Latin]] ''rufus'' meaning "rufous" with the genus ''[[Rallus]]'' that had been introduced by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1758 for the rails.<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Rufirallus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Rufirallus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=28 February 2025 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''fasciatus'' is [[Late Latin]] meaning "banded", from Latin ''fascia'' meaning "band" or "stripe"..<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=fasciatuse | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=fasciatus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=1 March 2025 }}</ref> The species is considered to be [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

==Description==
The black-banded crake is {{convert|17|to|20|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. The sexes are alike. Adults have a dark horn or blackish bill. Their head, throat, and breast are rufous. Their back, rump, and wings are deep olive-brown, with a light reddish tinge to the inner flight feathers. Their belly, vent, and undertail [[Covert feather|coverts]] are cinnamon-rufous with heavy black bars. Their legs and feet are bright coral red. Immatures have a paler head and breast than adults. Their upperparts' olive-brown has a chestnut wash and the barring on their underparts is olive-brown.<ref name=BBCR-BOW>{{cite web | last1=Schulenberg | first1=T.S. | last2=Kirwan | first2=G.M.| date=2024 | title=Black-banded Crake (''Rufirallus fasciatus''), version 1.1 | editor-last=Schulenberg | editor-first=T.S. | work=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.blbcra1.01.1  | access-date=12 June 2021 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> One author described the black-banded crake's song as "a rubbery, musical trill, deeper and more bubbly than that of [other] ''Laterallus'' crakes." The female sings a shorter version of the song than the male.<ref name=BBCR-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The black-banded crake is found in the western [[Amazon Basin]], from southeastern Colombia south through eastern Ecuador to south-central Peru and east into western Brazil. It inhabits humid landscapes with a dense understory such as [[secondary forest]], where it especially favors overgrown agricultural plots in tropical evergreen forest. It also favors thickets of ''[[Heliconia]]'' and, on river islands, stands of ''[[Cecropia]]''. In elevation it is mostly found below {{convert|600|m|ft|abbr=on}} but reaches as high as {{convert|1100|m|ft|abbr=on}} locally in Ecuador. The black-banded crake is a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=BBCR-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===
The black-banded crake forages on damp ground in dense cover, where it searches for food in leaf litter. Its diet has not been documented but is assumed to be invertebrates and seeds.<ref name=BBCR-BOW/>

===Breeding===
Almost nothing is known about the black-banded crake's breeding biology. It vigorously defends territories of up to about {{convert|2|ha|acre|sigfig=1|abbr=on}}. It makes a dome nest of grass with a side entrance. One was sited on a fallen limb about {{convert|2|m|ft|sigfig=1|abbr=on}} above the ground.<ref name=BBCR-BOW/>

==Conservation status==
The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) has assessed the black-banded crake as being of Least Concern. It has a large range but its population size and trend are not known. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> Authors describe it as "rare to locally fairly common" in Ecuador and "rare but widespread" in Peru. The species "may even benefit from low levels of human disturbance, taking advantage of edge habitats around small garden plots."<ref name=BBCR-BOW/>
